coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional inulin tethered silver-graphene quantum dots nanotheranostic module for pancreatic cancer therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Nigam Joshi, Preeti
Agawane, Sachin
Athalye, Meghana C.
Jadhav, Vrushali
Sarkar, Dhiman
Prakash, Rajiv
coredata.dc:description: Cancer nanotechnology is an emerging area of cancer diagnosis and therapy. Although considerable progress has been made for targeted drug delivery systems to deliver anticancer agents to particular site of interest, new nanomaterials are frequently being developed and explored for better drug delivery efficiency. In the present work, we have explored a novel nanoformulation based on silver-graphene quantum dots (Ag-GQDs) nanocomposite for its successful implementation for pancreatic cancer specific drug delivery in wistar rats. Carboxymethyl inulin (CMI); a modified variant of natural polysaccharide inulin is tethered with the nanocomposite via carbodiimide coupling to enhance the biocompatibility of nanoformulation. Experiments are performed to investigate the cytotoxicity reduction of silver nanoparticles after inulin tethering as well as anticancer efficacy of the system using 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU) as model drug. SEM, TEM, FT-IR, UV‐vis, photoluminescence and anti proliferative assays (MTT) are performed for characterisation of the nanocomposite. Hyaluronic acid (HA) is conjugated as targeting moiety for CD-44 (cancer stem cell marker) to fabricate a complete targeted drug delivery vehicle specific for pancreatic cancer. In the present work two prime objectives were achieved; mitigation the toxicity of silver nanoparticles by inulin coating and it's in vivo application for pancreatic cancer.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pancreatic cancer
Graphene quantum dots
Silver nanoparticle
Inulin
Nanocomposite
Targeted drug delivery

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots and silver – graphene composite
Functionalization of nanocomposite with CMI and hyaluronic acid
Loading of 5-FU and determination of loading efficiency
Pancreatic cancer in vivo studies on Wistar rats
In vivo study of anticancer activity of nanoformulation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of nanoformulation preparation and in vitro and in vivo theranostic application.
TEM images of (a) GQDs, (b) AgNPs, (c) CMI tethered GQD-Ag Nanocomposite.
A: FT-IR spectra of (1) GQDs (2) Ag-GQDs nanocomposite. B: XRD images of (1) GQDs, (2) GQD-Ag nanocomposite.
UV–vis spectra of (a) GQDs, (b) GQDs-Ag nanocomposite. Inset: UV vis spectra of Ag nanoparticles (signature peak of AgNPs at 430nm).
PL emission spectra of GQDs (a) for excitation wavelengths 330–380nm, (b) for excitation wavelengths 420–480nm.

In vitro imaging of Panc-1 cells at 20X magnification after 2 hour incubation at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 environment; (A, E) untreated cells in bright field, (B, F) DAPI stained cells, (C, G) cells incubated with GQDs-AgNPs and HA-GQDs-Ag NPs respectively, (D,H) merge images of B–C and F–G.

(a) MTT analysis of HA functionalized and un-functionalized formulations on Panc-1 cells. (A, B, C are the comparative % inhibition values for three experiments) (b) IC50 values of 5-FU on incubating Panc-1 cells with (1) free drug, (2) encapsulated in HA functionalized nanoformulation.

H&E staining of wistar rat pancreas: (a) control/healthy animals, (b) diseased control (B group), (c) animals treated with unfunctionalized nanoformulation (C group), (d) animals treated with HA functionalized nanoformulation.

H&E staining diagrams of liver, spleen, kidney, heart and lungs of A–D groups with no visible abnormalities. In B group (tumor induced untreated wistar rats) some congestion in the organs was observed (indicated by arrows) while organs of C& D (treated groups) were normal in appearance.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional inulin tethered silver-graphene quantum dots nanotheranostic module for pancreatic cancer therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Organic Chemistry Division, National Chemical Laboratory, Pune 411008, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Biochemical Sciences, National Chemical Laboratory, Pune 411008, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology (BHU), Varanasi, 200215, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cancer nanotechnology is an emerging area of cancer diagnosis and therapy. Although considerable progress has been made for targeted drug delivery systems to deliver anticancer agents to particular site of interest, new nanomaterials are frequently being developed and explored for better drug delivery efficiency. In the present work, we have explored a novel nanoformulation based on silver-graphene quantum dots (Ag-GQDs) nanocomposite for its successful implementation for pancreatic cancer specific drug delivery in wistar rats. Carboxymethyl inulin (CMI); a modified variant of natural polysaccharide inulin is tethered with the nanocomposite via carbodiimide coupling to enhance the biocompatibility of nanoformulation. Experiments are performed to investigate the cytotoxicity reduction of silver nanoparticles after inulin tethering as well as anticancer efficacy of the system using 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU) as model drug. SEM, TEM, FT-IR, UV‐vis, photoluminescence and anti proliferative assays (MTT) are performed for characterisation of the nanocomposite. Hyaluronic acid (HA) is conjugated as targeting moiety for CD-44 (cancer stem cell marker) to fabricate a complete targeted drug delivery vehicle specific for pancreatic cancer. In the present work two prime objectives were achieved; mitigation the toxicity of silver nanoparticles by inulin coating and it's in vivo application for pancreatic cancer.

ce:section.ce:para: Pancreatic cancer (PC) is still the deadliest cancer among all cancer related deaths worldwide with single digit 5year survival (1–7%) and approximately a quarter of a million people worldwide dying annually from this disease. Furthermore in developed countries, the incidences of pancreatic cancer have shown 2% annual rate of increase and in most of the cancers, the death rate has decreased significantly [1,2]. Late diagnosis and poor prognosis are the prime cause of concern in this fatal disease. Due to trivial symptoms, more than 50% of cases are diagnosed at metastatic and advance stages, while in 20% cases resection is the only option left. Apart from the complex vesicular structure and thick stromal architecture that prevents the effective delivery of anticancer drugs at target site, rapid metastasis and recurrence of pancreatic cancer after chemotherapy are the prime factors that make PC the deadliest of all cancers. Thus it is need of the hour to find new strategies to treat this difficult to handle malignancy.

‘Cancer nanomedicine’ is an emerging branch of nanotechnology and playing a pivotal role in cancer management with newer ideas and strategies for cancer treatments. Liposomal, polymeric, solid lipid nanoparticles and metal nanoparticles based drug delivery systems for different cancer like ovarian, breast, liver and prostate cancer have already been reported [3–5]. Delivery vehicles with better theranostic potential and efficiency are still required even after significant progress in the field of nanomedicine. Apart from conventional therapies for cancer treatment, newer methodologies have also been reported by researchers using nano-composites for drug delivery. Recently, Yuan et al. has reported a new delivery system based on gold-graphene nanocomposite for bioimaging and delivery of doxorubicin to HepG2 cells in vitro [6]. In another report, layered double hydroxide (LDH) nanocomposites are utilized for cancer drug delivery [7]. Calcium phosphate nanocomposite based delivery vehicle is reported by Kester et al. [8] Although significant progress has already been made in nano drug delivery systems yet very few reports are available on pancreatic cancer specific drug delivery. We have previously reported HSA-Graphene based an in vitro study of efficacy assessment of HSA-GQDs composite based drug delivery system for pancreatic cancer bioimaging and drug delivery [9]. Herein we demonstrate the application of metal-quantum dots nano-composite as a potential nanotheranostic platform for in vivo applications. We explored the optical properties of GQDs in vitro and in vivo and green synthesis route for the formation of Ag-GQDs nanocomposite. CMI is proven to be safe for in-vivo analysis and its tethering is beneficial to prevent non-specific absorption of nanocomposite [10].

Graphene quantum dots due to their excellent fluorescence properties and cost effectiveness are better substitutes of organic dyes, expensive fluorescent agents and semiconductor quantum dots. GQDs have a remarkable potential for their successful application in biomedicine owing to their excellent optical properties and excellent biocompatibility [11,12]. In this study, we have reported an inulin tethered nanocomposite to mitigate the toxicity of silver nanocomposite. Inulin is a biocompatible natural polysaccharide having fructose repeating units with applications in pharmaceutical and food industry. There is no previous report on a theranostic platform utilizing inulin tethered Ag-GQDs nanocomposite for in vivo drug delivery application for pancreatic cancer. Few reports affirm the use of dextran as coating agent to deal with the toxicity issue of silver nanoparticles but the degradation of dextran by dextranases enzymes is the major limiting factor for its wider applications. After degradation of dextran, the direct interaction of nanoparticles and blood components may result in their non specific accumulation and variations in surface chemistry that may reduce the efficacy of nanoformulation [13,14]. As compare to dextran, inulin is readily available, cannot be degraded by human body enzymes as it is exogenous to human body, and can be easily modified that increases its applicability. Moreover, inulin has low non-specific absorption in most tissues and can be easily filtered by kidneys [10]. A successful in vivo application of this nanoformulation for pancreatic cancer therapy proved the utility of the nanocomposite.

To develop a complete targeted drug delivery module, hyaluronic acid was conjugated with the nanocomposite. HA is biodegradable, biocompatible and non immunogenic glycosaminoglycan and is an essential component for cell growth and tissue organization. Many cancer cells express HA receptors like CD44 that make it an attractive entity for targeted drug deliver applications due to its specific affinity towards CD44 expressing cancer cells and ensure the efficient drug delivery at target site. HA is rich in functional groups, readily available for chemical conjugation with drugs and nanoparticles and in general, nanoformulations modified with HA exhibits better therapeutic efficiency [15,16]. We explored 5-FU as model drug to analyze the potential of nanocomposite as targeted drug delivery vehicle for pancreatic cancer in vitro and in vivo. From our knowledge of the existing literature, this is the first study that explored the multifunctional Ag-GQDs nanocomposite based targeted nanotheranostic platform for pancreatic cancer therapy in vivo. It also presents a generalized approach for extended use of this nanoformulation for other cancers as well.

5-Fluorouracil (5-FU), silver nitrate (AgNO3), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), H2SO4, Glutamine, mono/dibasic sodium phosphate salts, 1-ethyl 3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), sodium citrate were obtained from Sigma Aldrich and used as received. DMEM media was procured from Hi-Media and Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) was obtained from Gibco, India. Double distilled water was used to prepare the solutions at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots and silver – graphene composite
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots were synthesized by one step pyrolysis of natural amino acid glutamine. Similar methods have been earlier reported by Zhao et al. [17]. Briefly, 3.0g glutamine in a round bottom flask was heated up to 190–200°C with a heating mantle. In less than a minute, solid amino acid melted and the colourless boiling liquid turned brown, which confirmed the formation of GQDs. A 15.0ml water was added into the solution and kept under stirring condition for 45min. To remove the impurities, centrifugation was done at 13000rpm and supernatant was collected followed by freeze drying for further use. For preparing the GQDs-silver nanocomposite, 25ml of 15mg/ml GQDs was added into 50ml 1mM AgNO3 solution. Tri sodium citrate as reducing agent was used for synthesis of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) as described elsewhere [18,19]. Typically, sodium tricitrate was added drop wise into a 100°C heated solution with vigorous stirring till the colour of solution turned onto brownish red that indicated the formation of silver-GQDs nanocomposite.

Inulin was carboxymethylated (CMI) by a procedure described elsewhere [20]. Briefly, in a 3.0M inulin solution in NaOH, chloroacetic acid (1.8M) was added and the mixture was heated at 60°C for an hour. The reaction mixture was then cooled to room temperature; excess of ethanol was added and left overnight for precipitation. Dialysis in water was performed to eliminate unreacted compounds and impurities. The purified carboxymethylated inulin was dried in hot air oven and stored at room temperature for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of nanocomposite with CMI and hyaluronic acid
ce:section.ce:para: EDC-NHS coupling was used to link CMI with Ag-GQDs nanocomposite. For functionalization procedure, 5ml of 0.05M CMI solution was incubated for 1.5h in 3:1M ratio of EDC-NHS solution for activation of its carboxylic groups. Later 20ml nanocomposite suspension in PBS (pH7.0) was added to it and left overnight. Centrifugation was done at 13000rpm at room temperature followed by washing with distilled water to eliminate the unreacted compounds and impurities. The CMI tethering on nanocomposite can be a considered as core – shell architecture with Ag-GQDs core and polysaccharide shell.
Similar method was adopted for conjugating HA as targeting moiety on nanocomposite assuming not all the reactive functional groups will be conjugated with CMI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading of 5-FU and determination of loading efficiency
ce:section.ce:para: 5-Fluorouracil (10mg/ml) was the anticancer drug loaded on the nanocomposite via two step procedure. Firstly, we added 5-FU at tethering step to encapsulate it into the polysaccharide network followed by a surface adsorption step where 5mg/ml drug was added into nanoformulation suspension and left under mild stirring for 12h. The nano-drug formulation was centrifuged at 12000rpm to remove any unbound materials and kept at 0–4°C for further use.

The loading efficiency of 5-FU was measured via UV–vis spectrophotometer and analysis of different batches of drug loaded nanoformulation was carried out, which showed approximately 60–70% drug loading efficiency.

Loading efficiency can be calculated from:
(1)
Drug Encapsulation Efficiency
DEE
=
C td
−
C fd
/
C td
× 100
where, Ctd is concentration of total drug and Cfd is concentration of drug in supernatant.

To evaluate in vitro drug release profile of 5-FU from nanoparticles, 1.0mg nanoparticles were dispersed in 25ml of PBS buffer (pH7.4) and placed in shaker-incubator at 37°C (100rpm). At regular intervals, sampling was done (1ml solution was taken and replaced with same amount of buffer) and released amount was measured under UV–vis spectrophotometer.
(2)
5 − FU
%
release =
C ds
/
C td
× 100
where, Cds is the concentration of 5-FU released in the suspension and Ctd is the total amount of 5-FU encapsulated in the nanoparticles.

TEM (Tecnai, T-20, FEI, The Netherlands) images were taken to evaluate the structure and morphological details of GQDs and Ag-GQDs nanocomposites. A diluted sample of nanoparticle was drop casted on carbon coated copper grids and dried properly before study. Absorption spectra of the nanomaterial were obtained under UV–vis spectrophotometer (Cary50 Bio, Varian, The Netherlands) in the range of 200–800nm. Samples were measured in 1cm path length quartz cuvette was utilized for experiments at ~25°C. All the experiments were performed in triplicate. Similarly, FT-IR spectra of lyophilized samples of GQDs, AgNPs, nanocomposite and CMI were taken by FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet iS5, Thermo scientific, USA). The samples we converted into 1mm pellets with KBr and measured in the range of 500–4000cm−1. Photo Luminescence (LS 55, Perkin Elmer, USA) spectroscopy provided the variation in fluorescence properties of GQDs and nanocomposite between 400 and 800nm. The emission spectra were obtained for 360–480nm excitation wavelengths.

MTT (3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-3,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay was performed to analyze the anticancer activity of our nanoformulation as well as for the biocompatibility of nanocomposite. The assay protocol was same as described by us previously [9]. Briefly, Panc-1 cells (pancreatic cancer cell line) were seeded at a density of 1×104 cells/well in 96 well plates in DMEM media with 10% FBS and 50μl gentamicin at 37°C in 5% CO2 environment for 24h. Cells were more than 65–70% confluent at the time of addition of drug loaded functionalized and unfunctionalized nanoformulations further incubated for 48h into the same environment. After second incubation, MTT (10μl, 10mg/ml) was added and cells were incubated again for 4h for the reaction to take place. As a next step, culture media was removed and DMSO (150μl) was added followed by measurement of optical density (OD) at 570nm after 15min incubation with shaking. All the experiments were performed in triplicate to avoid experimental errors.

To determine the biocompatibility of Ag-GQDs composite and effect of tethering with CMI, MTT assay was performed with three cells lines (HeLa (Cervix), HepG2 (Liver) and Panc-1) with the same procedure as mentioned above. Cells were incubated with relatively high concentrations (50–150μg/ml) of CMI coated and uncoated nanocomposite.

For in vitro imaging, Panc-1 (Pancreatic cancer cell line) cells were incubated in 50μl of HA-Ag-GQDs and Ag-GQDs suspensions for 2h in a six well plate in DMEM media supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum in 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37°C. Cellular uptake of nanoformulation was visualized by Evos cell imaging system, Invitrogen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pancreatic cancer in vivo studies on Wistar rats
ce:section.ce:para: Wistar rats of average body weight (wt) 250–300g were given intraperitoneal injections (IP) of 5×105 Panc-1 cell suspensions in PBS (pH7.4) at the site of pancreas once in fifteen days for tumor generation. After successful tumor induction animals were randomly grouped into three groups (B, C, D with six animals in each group while healthy wistar rats were kept in group ‘A’ consist of six animals). 10mg/kg intraperitoneal dose of 5-FU loaded, HA functionalized and unfunctionalized CMI-Ag-GQDs nanoformulation was given twice in a week and later once in a week for second and third week to two groups (C, D respectively) except the control (B) which contained untreated wistar rats. Animals were sacrificed after a month for histopathology examination to confirm tumor induction and to investigate the effect of treatment in treated wistar rats.

The animals were sacrificed on the last day of experiment and examined for gross lesions. Parts of spleen, liver, heart, lungs, kidney, pancreas and brain were fixed in 10% formalin for preparation of histological slides and were processed in a Leica TP 1020 tissue processor. Tissues were embedded in paraffin blocks using Leica EG 1160 paraffin embedder and cut into ribbons of 4mm using a Microm HM 360 microtome. Hemotoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining of samples were done using a Microm HMS-70 stainer. The permanent slides were made and evaluated for histopathological changes under Olympus BX51 microscope. The slides were properly coded to avoid possible bias before analysis.

These studies were carried out according to the guidelines for the care and use of laboratory animals and experimental protocols and approved by Institutional Animals Ethics Committee of Symbiosis International University, Pune.
Fig. 1 is the diagrammatic representation of the steps involved in the synthesis of nanocomposite as well its theranostic application in vitro and in vivo.

In vivo bioimaging analysis was performed with Xenogen Ivis Live imaging system (NCCS-Pune), Perkin Elmer. Wistar rats from each group were anesthetized and given IP injection of 10mg/kg of nanocomposite to detect the in vivo imaging potential of graphene quantum dots at excitation wavelength of 460nm for better penetration.

In this study, green route of synthesis is explored for formation of silver nanoparticles-GQDs hybrid. GQDs were prepared by a one step pyrolysis procedure using glutamine as precursor. A bottom up approach of synthesis for silver-quantum dots hybrid was adopted via reduction of silver nitrate in the solution of tri-sodium citrate comprising of GQDs. The electrostatic interaction between the active hydroxyl and carboxyl groups presents on surface and edges of GQDs resulted the formation of Ag-GQDs hybrid. The citric acid played an important role as capping agents that stabilized the nano-hybrids. The presence of functional groups of GQDs was an added advantage of this synthesis that enhanced their applicability due to ease of modification. Herein we have coupled carboxymethyl inulin to Ag-GQDs hybrid by EDC-NHS carbodiimide coupling. TEM image (Fig. 2a, b) revealed the morphology and size of GQDs, Ag-GQDs. The size of nanocomposite particles was in the range of 10–35nm due to AgNPs on GQDs surface. (Fig. 2b) In TEM image (2c), the nanocomposite containing CMI is clearly visible in the form of sheet like structures. Inulin (an exogenous polysaccharide) was chemically modified to generate carboxymethyl groups that played an important role in enhancing the functionality of inulin. Fig. 3 shows the illustration of the FTIR spectra of modified inulin. A broad peak ~3431cm−1 in Fig. 3a corresponds to hydroxyl (OH) stretching of glucose and fructose while peaks at 2832cm−1 and peaks between 1300 and 1500cm−1 that are characteristics peaks of aliphatic groups (CH and CH2) in inulin. The bands at ~1590 and 1400cm−1 attributed to the anti-symmetric and asymmetric stretching modes of the carboxylic group (COO–) and these peaks confirm the functionalization of inulin polysaccharide chain.CMI was utilized for tethering due to its well proven biocompatibility, easy degradability and low non specific absorption that is an important trait for effective in vivo application of nanoformulation. These results were in accordance with the previous report of CMI functionalized magnetic nanoparticles [21].

Silver nanoparticles have some toxicity associated with them but with presence of GQDs and CMI tethering, we tried to compensate this issue. The coated nanocomposite exhibited less cytotoxicity as compared to uncoated one that enabled us to apply the nanoformulation for in vivo drug delivery application. Fig. 4A represents the FTIR spectra of GQDs, and Ag-GQDs hybrid. GQDs have abundance of amino (NH2), carboxyl (COOH) and hydroxy (OH) groups on their surface and edges responsible for the excellent hydrophobicity of GQDs. While interacting with AgNPs, CO groups got reduced and as exhibited in the FT-IR spectra, a reduced intensity of peaks of oxygen containing groups in the spectra of GQDs (1160cm−1 corresponding to CO groups) clearly indicated interaction of AgNPs with quantum dots reactive groups. The FTIR spectrum of CMI and HA functionalized nanocomposite exhibited a new peak at ~1735–1750cm−1 corresponding to CONH carbodiimide band stretching that confirms the CMI and HA modification of the nanocomposite (data not shown). XRD spectra also supported the hypothesis of interaction of GQDs and AgNPs in nanocomposite. As exhibited in Fig. 4B, apart from a broad characteristic peak of GQDs (002) centered at 2θ of ~25° (Fig 4B-a) a slight variation in 2θ was exhibited with (002) peak shifted to 25.7°. As reported by Son et al. these changes in 2θ values is related with the interlayer spacing that itself is associated with percentage of carbon [22]. An increase in 2θ value indicates decrease in interlayer spacing and can be attributed to decrease of oxygen containing groups due to interaction of GQDS and silver nanoparticles. The characteristic peaks at 2θ=~37°, 46°, 66, and 78° representing bands of (111), (200), (220), and (311) crystalline planes of Ag is a clear indication of the formation of pure crystalline Ag on the GQDs surface [23]. (Fig. 4B-b) Colour of GQD solution was yellow initially while Ag-GQDs nanocomposite appeared yellow-brown. UV–vis spectra exhibited characteristic peaks of GQDs at 240nm and 340nm. (Fig. 5 ) The peak at 240nm was attributed to the π-π* transitions of CC bonds while peak at 340nm indicated the presence of sp2 clusters. A signature peak of Ag NPs at ~430nm along with the other peaks of GQDs in the UV spectra of nanocomposite was another proof of Ag nanoparticles interaction with GQDs. Photoluminescence properties of GQDs are the most important feature for their potential use as nanotheranostic platform. The as synthesized GQDs showed an excitation dependent behaviour and varying the excitation wavelength from 330 to 500nm the emission spectra shifted towards larger wavelengths. The PL spectra of GQDs showed emission at ~430nm with excitation between 330 and 380nm (Fig. 6a) while on excitation with 420–500nm, an emission peak at ~530nm was obtained that was the origin of green fluorescence of GQDs. (Fig. 6b) Although after functionalization of nanocomposite with CMI and HA, there was a decrease in PL intensity of GQDs but fluorescence was sufficient enough to utilize the nanoformulation for in vitro and in vivo imaging experiments as illustrated in Fig. S1.

Surface charge or zeta potential of nanocomposite was ~−26.12mV (Fig. S2). Carboxylic groups on HA and CMI are prima facie responsible for the negative charge on nanocomposite due to the presence of deprotonated carboxy groups at physiological pH.

Graphene quantum dots exhibit excellent fluorescence properties. Herein, the nano composite also showed stable green fluorescence that was explored for in vitro and in vivo imaging of cancer cells. Panc-1 cells were incubated with nanocomposite and imaging was done after 2h. Fig. 7 illustrates the results of in vitro imaging of panc-1 cells after illuminating with 460nm laser source. Cells that received HA functionalized GQDs nanocomposites showed better fluorescence as compared to unfunctionalized GQDs (Fig. 7C, G) due to better accumulation of nanoparticles on CD44 positive panc-1 cells; target moiety for HA. While without HA functionalization a lesser quantity of nanocomposite was internalized into the cells.

We investigated the in vivo imaging efficacy of nanocomposite in wistar rats. Although targeted tumor bioimaging with GQDs have also been reported and due to the presence of HA, our multifunctional nanocomposite can also be explore for targeted bioimaging, in this analysis we have investigated the stability of fluorescence in vivo. GQDs dosage of 10mg/kg body wt of the animals was administered and imaging was done after 30min. The fluorescence images were captured at 360nm and 460nm. Due to better penetration abilities of long wavelengths, experiments were performed at 460nm excitation. PL intensity was taken at different time intervals and after 1.5h the intensity got diminished probably due to circulation of blood. Fig. S3 is exhibiting time dependent bioimaging via GQDs. This analysis demonstrated that the in-vivo fluorescence imaging experiments for short durations of 1–1.5h in wistar rats under intravenous injections can be explored for superficial tissue imaging at 530–540nm emission.

The inulin tethered nanocomposite was prepared via green route of synthesis. The cytotoxity of the Ag-GQDs composite was tested for three cell lines (Panc-1, HepG2 and HeLa) and it is clearly evident in Fig. S4 that carboxymethyl inulin coated Ag-GQDs composite was more biocompatible than uncoated nanocomposite.

To prepare anticancer drug loaded nanoformulation, 5-FU (10mg/ml) was loaded in to Ag-GQDs composite during the inulin functionalization step to encapsulate the drug into the CMI-Ag-GQDs nanocomposite. To enhance the loading capacity of drug, physical absorption was also performed. UV–vis analysis was performed to quantify drug loading and the data revealed that approximately 60–70% drug loading efficiency was obtained for different formulations (data not shown). In vitro drug release was determined by UV–vis analysis at 270nm and in approximately 18–20h all the drug was released from the nanocomposite. (Fig. S5) MTT assay was performed to investigate the antiproliferative efficacy of nanoformulation. The nanocomposite was functionalized with HA via EDC-NHS coupling reaction. HA is targeting moiety for CD44 cancer stem cell marker that is over expressed on pancreatic cancer cells. We examined the efficacy of drug loaded formulation only for Panc-1 cell lines to perform specificity analysis of nanoformulation for pancreatic cancer. Data obtained from the MTT assay revealed that HA functionalized formulation was more effective in cell inhibition (72–78% inhibition) than unfunctionalized nanoformulation (~56%). (Fig. 8a) An IC50 value of 1.1μg/ml was obtained with free 5-FU while in nanoformulation, that further got reduced to 0.68μg/ml. This reduction in IC50 values can be explained by the target-receptor interaction on panc-1 cell surface. Affinity of HA with CD44 cell receptor was the prime factor for the targeted drug delivery and better internalization of anti cancer drug to cancer cells due to receptor mediated endocytosis that ensure better therapeutic efficacy of the nanoformulation as depicted in Fig. 8b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo study of anticancer activity of nanoformulation
ce:section.ce:para: For in vivo examination to test the efficacy of nanoformulation, we adopted a much simpler approach of pancreatic cancer induction. Panc-1 cells (5×105 cells/ml in PBS buffer, pH7.0) were directly injected at the site of rat pancreas twice with an interval of 15days. After tumor induction, an IP dosage of 10mg/ml of animal body weight of 5-FU loaded HA unfunctionalized and functionalized nanoformulation was given twice a week to animals of ‘C’ and ‘D’ groups respectively. In H&E staining of untreated group-B, many viable cells were visible (Fig. 9b) while for HA-Ag-GQDs-5FU and Ag-GQDs-5FU treated groups (Fig. 9c) a reduction in viable cells was observed. However no evidence of tumor cells was observed with functionalized nanoformulation treated group as illustrated in Fig. 9(d). That clearly indicated that the payload was efficiently delivered at target site of action. Moreover, in comparison to conventional chemotherapy, no cytotoxicity and cytological changes to other organs were observed. Fig. 10 represents the H&E staining slides of other organs (kidney, spleen, liver, heart and lungs). Apparently there were no visible sign of any abnormalities or lesions produced during the entire regimen. In B group animals as shown, some congestion was observed that was probably be due to tumor growth in pancreas but the organs of treated groups (C&D) exhibited no morphological anomalies. These findings clearly proved the biocompatibility of the nanoformulation.

The field of cancer biology has been greatly benefited by the new inventions of nanotechnology and a new area has been emerged as cancer nanotechnology & nanomedicine. Pancreatic cancer is a difficult to handle malignancy and gemcitabine is still the ultimate treatment strategy due to the late diagnosis of cancer in advance stages where no therapy option except chemotherapy is left. Chemotherapy itself suffers with major drawbacks of nonspecific absorption and delivery of low dose of drug to target site. Although newer treatment options have been explored by researchers but the success rate is not high.

Nanomedicine has opened new vistas for cancer chemotherapy with its targeted drug delivery approaches that ensures specific absorption of nano-cargo at tumor site with high pay load delivery. We have designed a multifunctional versatile theranostic platform for drug delivery and bioimaging and explored its potential for pancreatic cancer specifically. The outcome of this study established the applicability of green synthesized inulin tethered Ag-GQDs nanocomposite in drug delivery applications. Toxicity of nanomaterials s a major cause of concern for their in vivo applications and reports are there on mitigating the toxicity of nanoparticles by modified synthesis procedures [24–28]. Herein, the issues related with stability and cytotoxicity of AgNPs were dealt with simple cost effective eco friendly modification of nanocomposite with carboxymethyl inulin. We put forth a new idea of mitigating the cytotoxicity of silver nanoparticles by tethering of biocompatible CMI on the nanocomposite and as shown in Fig. S4, results of MTT assay supported our claim that CMI conjugation on GQD-Ag nanocomposite significantly reduces the cytotoxicity of the nanoformulation. It was also modified with HA to achieve better therapeutic efficiency at target site in vitro and in vivo as HA is the targeting moiety for CD-44 receptors over expressed at pancreatic cancer cells, which is helpful for better internalization of drug. Mechanism of this phenomenon can be explained by receptor mediated endocytosis [29]. Enhanced permeability and retention effect (EPR) might also play a vital role in the accumulation of more GQD-HA in cancer cells. The in vitro imaging results obtained were very encouraging to be considered for in vivo bioimaging applications of the nanoformulation. The stable fluorescence intensity of the composite for an hour was significantly good for in vivo analysis.

Zeta potential is an important criterion to determine the surface charge of nanoparticles that establishes the stability and absorption of nanoparticles at target site. Due to the presence of HA and CMI, zeta potential of the nanoformulation was negative and its reported that typically, nanoparticles with zeta potential greater than +25mV or less than −25mV exhibits high degree of stability as more surface charge on nanoparticles prevent their aggregation that in turn enhances the stability of nano suspension [30]. Moreover negative charges on the surface of nanoparticles prevent the non specific absorption of nanoparticles on cells [31].

This nanoformulation incorporated 5-FU as model drug to prove the therapeutic efficacy of nanoformulation. 5-FU is principally a thymidylate synthase (TS) inhibitor and blocks the synthesis of the pyrimidine thymidine, which is a nucleoside required for DNA replication. Thymidylate synthase methylates deoxyuridine monophosphate (dUMP) to form thymidine monophosphate (dTMP) [32]. 5-FU causes a scarcity in dTMP that triggers cell death of rapidly dividing cancerous cells via thymine less death. MTT assay was performed to investigate in-vitro efficacy of nanoformulation and later we performed the in vivo studies to validate the efficacy of nanoformulation. As clearly indicated in the histopathology data, the nanotheranostic module was effective as a therapeutic agent for cancer and in the treated groups (C & D) significant reduction in cancer cells was observed in comparison to untreated rats in group ‘B’. Apart from that, it is also worth noticing that the nanocomposite was biocompatible and no abnormalities were observed in other tissues and organs as evidenced from the H&E staining of kidney, liver, spleen, lung and heart.

The purpose of this study was to design an efficient nanotheranostic system for targeted drug delivery based on graphene quantum dots. The results of this study indicated that conjugation of inulin; a biocompatible polysaccharide can be applied as surface modifier for metal nanoparticles to mitigate their toxicity. This is an important outcome of this study as to the best of our knowledge; this is the first in vivo study of inulin modified nanoformulation. Based on the encouraging in vitro and in vivo results of inhibition of pancreatic cancer cells growth, it can be concluded that the nanovector was biocompatible and efficiently delivered the payload at target site.

This study is the first demonstration of inulin tethered nano framework of Ag-GQDs nanocomposite with its potential use as drug delivery vehicle for pancreatic cancer. AgNPs are relatively toxic but tethering of carboxymethyl inulin significantly mitigated the cytotoxicity of AgNPs and it is one of the major outcomes of this study. 5-FU as model anti cancer drug was loaded and this HA functionalized nano-composite was tested against Panc-1 cell line and pancreatic cancer animal model. Based on the experimental data obtained, it can be concluded that the nanocomposite has the potential to be applied as a versatile nanotheranostic platform for drug delivery and bioimaging. The presence of functional groups is advantageous for the possible modification of nanocomposite with a variety of target molecules for cell surface receptors specific for different cancers.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author Dr. Preeti Nigam Joshi is thankful to Department of Science and Technology, Govt of India for INSPIRE Award Grant to carry out this research work.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2017.03.176.
